original_abstract: This research highlights the view of Islamic family law on the rejection of the marriage contract of LGBT couples as a marriage, which is seen through the lens of Maslahah theory. Through a literature study using qualitative research based on the scalpel of Maslahah theory, The research underlines that marriage in Islam is not only an agreement between two individuals but also an institution governed by certain conditions and pillars, including the existence of male and female parties who are permitted according to Sharia, as well as the existence of a guardian, dowry, and fair witnesses. In the context of LGBT couples, the marriage contract does not fulfil these conditions, so it cannot be considered a marriage contract in Islamic family law. In addition, the rejection is analysed through Maslahah theory, which emphasises the importance of maintaining the integrity of the family structure, moral values and spiritual goals in society. The refusal is interpreted as an attempt to preserve the general welfare by maintaining social harmony and adherence to Islamic principles. It shows that the rejection of marriage contracts for LGBT couples is not discriminatory but rather the result of a deep understanding of the concept of marriage in Islam, with the aim of achieving broader welfare and protecting the institution of the family as the basic social and spiritual unit. This research confirms that Islamic law is a dynamic and adaptive system seeking to promote justice and welfare in accordance with religious principles and the needs of society. Thus, the rejection of marriage contracts for LGBT couples in Islamic family law can be understood as part of an effort to maintain the harmony of society without abandoning the underlying religious principles.
humanized_abstract: Title: THE OPPOSITION AGAINST THE MARRIAGE CONTRACT OF LGBT COUPLES AS A MARRIAGE IN ISLAMIC FAMILY LAW BASED ON MAZLAHAH THEORY Background: Recently, there has been a lot of discussion regarding the marriage contract of LGBT couples as a marriage in Islamic family law, with many Muslims opposing it. From the view of Islamic family law, they consider the opposition as a correct and just decision. Based on Maslahah theory, the purpose of the law is to fulfill the benefits of the society. Methods: The research method used is literature study with qualitative approach using Maslahah scalpel. Discussion: Marriage in Islam is not a contract between two individuals but also it is an institution with conditions and pillars. Some of the conditions and pillars of marriage in Islam are such as the existence of male and female parties that are allowed in Sharia, a guardian, dowry, and fair witnesses. When LGBT couples enter into a marriage contract, the marriage contract does not fulfill those conditions. Therefore, the marriage contract of LGBT couples cannot be called a marriage contract in Islamic family law. In addition, the opposition against the marriage contract of LGBT couples as a marriage in Islamic family law can be explained by using Maslahah theory. Maslahah theory itself is used to determine and know the benefit of the law. In Islam, the law that is considered to give benefits to mankind is called as Maslahah Al Mushlaahah, which consists of four basic components. Those are, the protection of the structure of the family institution, the upholding of morality, and the realization of the goal of religion. Thus, the opposition against the marriage contract of LGBT couples as a marriage in Islamic family law is to maintain the Maslahah Al Mushlaahah which will bring a greater benefit to the society, such as the preservation of the social harmony, and upholding the laws of Islam. Conclusion: Islamic law is dynamic law that has the ability to accommodate changes of time, while still maintaining the justice and benefit of the society. Based on the research, it can be concluded that the opposition against the marriage contract of LGBT couples as a marriage in Islamic family law is not discrimination, but it is a deeper understanding of marriage in Islam and to fulfill the welfare and the benefit of the society, especially in preserving the institution of family as the basic social and spiritual institution in the society.
